content.problemStatement:
**Arrays** are used to store multiple items of the same datatype in a single variable.
This course assumes you already know how to use arrays in your preferred language.

In case you don't please follow the appropriate courses - [C](https://www.codechef.com/learn/course/c), [C++](https://www.codechef.com/learn/course/cpp), [Python](https://www.codechef.com/learn/course/python), [Java](https://www.codechef.com/learn/course/java)

After you have made sure you know the basics of `Arrays` in your preferred language, we will talk about `Array` as a data structure.   
Some properties of Arrays are:
- **`Array`** is a linear data structure, meaning all elements are arranged sequentially, one after another.
- **`Array`** elements are stored in contiguous memory locations, meaning even in memory locations all the array elements are stored one after the other, this makes it easy to predict at which memory location any array element is stored. Thus we can directly access or update any array element in $O(1)$ time.
- Since all the elements are stored in a contiguous manner, inserting or deleting any element means we have to shift all the elements after the desired element, making it an $O(N)$ operation.  
Let's gain a bit more understanding by performing these operations on an array.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
